text: The surgeon performs piecemeal dissection of the triangle of Calot, alternating between anterior and posterior views to manage moderate inflammation. Approximately one third of the gallbladder fossa is dissected before clipping or cutting any structures to ensure no aberrant anatomy is present.
The cholangiogram catheter is inserted into the abdomen through a 14 French angiocatheter placed in the epigastric region, visible as a small needle puncture without requiring an incision.

The surgeon places one clip on the distal cystic duct and two clips on the cystic artery. Using hook cautery, a ductotomy is created. A third clip is placed on the artery, and the catheter is maneuvered into the duct using an empty clip applier. The intraoperative cholangiogram is performed by undocking one robotic arm, followed by placement of proximal cystic duct clips.

The surgeon transects the cystic duct and cystic artery, then removes the gallbladder from the fossa using electrocautery. The abdominal wall is closed in a standard manner, and the angiocatheter puncture site is sealed with Dermabond or Steristrips.
